commentBody: Early returns at xwstats call this puzzle Very Hard.  That may change, but it tells me that my experience was not unusual.  I thought maybe I was just tired.

Nice Friday offering from Kelly. No rebuses to bother people. No theme for people to not like. Just plain old clues and answers.
I hope this one doesn't cause a STIR. And I don't see NCWYETH anywhere in the grid.
Never heard of dealer's shoe, Elise Neal, or used the word "goose" to mean energizes. Sometimes the puzzle giveth and sometimes...

THEWAYILOOKATIT this was a nice sticky Friday. Although it had more general knowledge questions than I like, I was able to MAKEITMAKESENSE.  


I seldom quibble about the accuracy of clues, but as a lawyer who did a fair amount of bankruptcy work, I found REOPENED to be a little iffy.  In the vast majority of Chapter 11 cases the business remains open throughout the bankruptcy, or if it does shut down, it either remains shut down or reopens before “emerging” from bankruptcy. I concede there may be cases that fit the clue, but the answer ran counter to my experience, and I’m feeling mildly churlish tonight
Tough (for me at least) but fair. A satisfying solve. And I learned that the lucite thingy on a blackjack table has a name. A weird, nonsensical name.

1.  Took me about the normal time, but a bit longer
2.  I really sweat out a couple of areas
3.  GOOSES is hilarious, and it took me forever to see it
4.  Never heard of MEWS, CAVA, ANNA Hedgeman, or ELISE Neal
5.  22A is HERE just like every other clue/answer
6.  Not sure why those are IOTAS in 27D.

For all these reasons, and some that I've forgotten already, I declare this puzzle to be Friday-worthy.

If you wish to argue with me, I'd point you towards 31D.

I have fallen asleep weeping and praying for 14A for nearly 10 years now.

I don't think I'm going to get an answer.

I love the spanners! They nicely form opposing pairs: 14A and 17A reflect a desire to know and understand, while 49A and 52A reflect a desire to blind and occlude.

Also, someone who FLIES SOLO ain't helping with the EMISSIONS problem. Just saying.
Had I been solving in pen, this puzzle would have required a bottle of white-out.
Best clever clue and answer: 22 ACROSS.

I may be in the minority but mundanely worded spanners like 17A and 52A bore the heck out me. There's no aha moment when they fall into place. OTOH, I now know about potoroos and that was kind of fun.
PLANE figures are specifically shapes in two dimensions, or flat. ... But I never understood how they got snakes on a plane.

Is it me or am I seeing a lot of IOTAS in this puzzle? Could be renamed “The Ego’s Solution.”

Was able to hash out the spanners quite easily and lost my sense of direction in the Mideast. 

Very well done.
Def a Saturday like puzzle, especially with all the phrases in the solution!

@Sean. While it might “seem obvious in retrospect” the [blue] signifies LEFT to you, for most of the world it is red that signifies left and blue right in politics.  Fortunately,I’ve seen enough American political news to get the answer
That was hard!  Way over my average.
I want to hear more about the puzzle hunt at Prospect Park. Lived nearby decades ago and think such an event would be fun in my PNW  town. We have great parks system and community support.

I am so impressed!!!  I mean I am really wowed!!!

Yes, of course for this Friday-worthy puzzle, and the brilliant mazes of meanings that Kelly Morenus cooked up, but for me as well!  I finished it with no look-ups, no hints, nothing but teeth grinding grit, with feints and  jabs and dancing backward, pleading MAKE IT MAKE SENSE!!  

(If anyone thought this was easy, I DON'T WANT TO KNOW!)

The bank only gave me bills and no coins. Make it make cents!

(I always mint to post that.)
Am I the only one who hates that the article now opens in the game app’s internal browser with no option to open in external browser?

I don’t know if I found this hard or found this easy. But It was fun. The spanners were great. 
Because imma degenerate, DEALERSSHOE was a gimme. But it did seem pretty “inside baseball”. 


REOPENED seems kinda weird to me for “came out of bankruptcy”. I had solvency for a long time as a placeholder. Though the tense seemed to not match.  

Anyway. Nice Friday.  Looking forward Saturday. Hopefully it’s a killer

The cluster of GOOSES, DEVO and POTOROO - all total unknowns - defeated me, especially as I was unfamiliar with DEALER'S [SHOE], too, and the biblical verb and LITE didn't come to me as easily as they probably should have. I was only able to resolve that mess with lookups of the marsupial and the band, and ultimately with a puzzle check.

That's too bad, because otherwise the puzzle was enjoyably challenging. My first across and down pass yielded very little, but then I managed to tune into the clues and the solve progressed nicely. Then I hit that wall...

It always feels uncool to be bested not by clever clueing but rather by personal arcana. That one area of the grid had 0 fun factor for me.

I would agree with xwstats to put this in the Very Hard category. It was a bear for me, about 33% over my Friday average. The East area took the most time. 

It was fun figuring out the spanners. There’s often that gnawing thought early on that maybe I won’t be able to fill this one in. But I put that aside and tell myself that I have a lot of time, I can always lay it down and come back to it and maybe see things differently. 

It was a good workout and a formidable Friday.  Thanks for the experience, Kelly.

Top notch Friday puzzle. I needed loads of crossers to get almost every answer, but gradually with trial & error and educated guesswork, they came. Had to really massage the long spanners to get them to reveal themselves, but again it was satisfying as they slowly stepped out from the shadows.
Avoiding frustration and despair as square after square goes unfilled takes a lot of patience, but it really is worth it in the end!
Thanks Kelly!

Is it only in the US that red is right and blue is LEFT ? 

Certainly in every other country whose politics I’m aware of pink, red, green are progressive (centre to left) and black, blue, yellow are more conservative (right). 

A nice puzzle. A few trivia that had to be inferred but that’s part and parcel of my solve since I’m lousy at retaining useless information.
Fujis aren't really red apples. Otherwise not too bad.

From the Wordplay column: "26D. I needed to ask a puzzle editor to clarify the clue [Kind of figure], and she was kind enough to educate me about PLANE figures, a geometric term that essentially just refers to shapes. No matter what you encounter this Friday, rest easy knowing that you didn’t have to ask a puzzle editor at The New York Times to explain shapes to you."

PLANE figures are two dimensional. You can draw them on a paper, like circles, rectangles and squares, triangles, etc. 

By contrast, solid figures are three dimensional: cubes, pyramids, cones, spheres, cylinders, etc. They are something you can hold in your hand. 

Voilà.

When puzzles miss the mark by a little, as I feel this one did, I feel it's the editor who should take the blame. Reading the completing puzzle, everything makes perfect sense. You need the outside perspective to point out the flaws, and this clearly did not happen in this case.

In the UK, mews (which can refer to a single building or multiple, attached buildings) is not a word for "row houses".   Mews are former stable/carriage houses from Georgian and Victorian times, and they do not front onto streets.  They are in alleyways or courtyards behind both commercial and residential buildings, or behind individual homes or businesses. They were the rear garages of their time many of them have been converted into really lovely residences which have retained their original stable doors.

Row houses--which front onto streets---are called terraces or terraced houses.

in the UK if a place is called a mews, it really was stables.   In North America, 20th century property developers began naming roads of newly-built regular street-fronting rows of attached or semi-detached (and horse-free) houses "Mews" because it has a charming, olde-worlde sound to it.
Very difficult 2026 Friday.
I wish we could rate puzzles because this one would get only one star if I was feeling generous.

I finished this considerably faster than my average Friday time (always with no research or lookups). Don't ask me why. Sometimes you're simply on the constructor's wavelength,  I guess.

As an added plus, I now know what a "Cool S" is and how to draw it! Thank you, Sean!
Parasailing is not a sport. It is a recreational activity.

We had a discussion here a while ago about MEWS. In the UK row houses are called a terrace, and definitely not a MEWS.

This was hard. Even when I revealed or looked up some clues I wasn't enlightened. You snap on DIAPERS? My babies' nappies used sticky tape, and before that - a large safety pin. 
PEEN, RED APPLE, HOGANS, GOOSES, POTOROO, the SHOE .... 

But that's fine - I'm glad others had the challenging Friday they expect.

Well, I started out thinking this would be a romp.  The first two spanners went in with one cross each, so the top third basically filled itself in.

The rest was nice and crunchy.  Since I already had RED APPLE, I thought aPlomb fit in nicely at 36 D.  That really messed me up for a while.  If it wasn't such a nice word, I probably would have taken it out sooner, but it just looked so good there that it took a while before I gave it another look.

The SE came together slowly but surely, DEVO being the key that opened it up.

Very nice, Kelly.  Thanks for a fun Friday.
I really enjoyed this one.  It didn’t come easy, but eventually fell together.   That area with IOTAS came last.  Learned what MEWS are.

Like Sean, without being spotted the letters, I would have been lost on POTOROO. It was grand knowing that the crossings contained one of those four letters. The P in PHASES was the last square in the puzzle for me.

As others have noted, MEWS wasn't really correct, but in the "it's a Friday crossword" spirit, I figured that was what the constructor (or editor who changed the clue) was going for.

POTOROO definitely tripped me up, but was fairly confident in crosses in the bottom half and the starting letter because of PHASES.  Being given the letters definitely helped.  IOTAS definitely did not get me, I have seen this type of clue before with chis and etas.

That was beyond difficult, with clues that felt particularly US based, making it an almost impossible fill for this International solver: RUN INTERFERENCE, HOGANS, particularly unknowable. As for MEWS. Others have already explained how inaccurate this clue is so I won’t go on, but it’s not the first time this clue/answer combo has been used, so come on Ed, a little tighter on the checks please.

I’m currently changing multiple nappies (DIAPERS) for my baby grandson. There are no snap on varieties available here, it’s sticky tapes around the waistband, just as it was 30+ years ago when mine were tinies. I’d love to know exactly what snaps onto what.

So, that’s my whinge over with. I hate being so down on a puzzle just because I’ve struggled with it and am always in awe of the constructors skill, but sometimes a puzzle feels exclusionary (if that’s the right word) if you’re not a part of the culture, rather than being delightfully difficult and an opportunity to fill in gaps in your general knowledge. Like TIL POTOROO. 

Final nit; is it my iPad playing up or has the Wordplay format changed? The comments section has moved to a separate page, rather than integrated with Wordplay. Can’t say it’s an improvement.
I found this one enjoyably tough today. Took far, far longer than my Friday average.

One of the signature Crosslandia moments is when you revisit an area numerous times and still can’t crack it; it still looks the same, you haven’t put in anything new in ages.  But then, on the nth time a fissure opens somewhere inside you and out bubbles an answer you know is right, the tension dissolves, and you sit with gratitude and wonder at how that happens.  Often that one word triggers a splat-fill that breaks open a huge area.

Is there a term for that moment?

I bring this all up because I was in true stuck-ness several times today, and these wondrous life rafts did come, just when I thought they never would.

This was, for me, a tough puzzle punctuated by these feel-good moments, created by a constructor who is skilled at making grids that RUN INTERFERENCE.  I love working hard through difficult grids like this, grids that enable these wondrous pops of pleasure.  Thank you, and more like this please, Kelly!

Not feeling particularly clever today.

This took forrrrever.

Gonna need to read what y’all have to say
about shoes and peens and some other bits
to make sense of the happy music.

I got it,
but I don’t feel like I deserved it.

But I do hope and pray
that Friday will give me trouble,

and it did.

Fo. Sho.

Awesome.

Advice to constructors: Ignore the whiners. 

Congratulations, Kelly!

It's fair to say that I struck out on my own while solving this puzzle. Though I saw a few things that eventually would float up from the dregs of my memory,  ISIS*, SETH*, CAVA, I didn't feel confident filling in anything on my first pass until I got to the DEVO/DRNO  combo all the way in SE corner. 
It was a proper Friday struggle for me. 

*Noticed after the fact that these two are names of Egyptian gods, which per wiki were sister and brother.

This was strangely hard for me too but I can’t put my finger on it. Nothing flashy or obscure (except POTOROO which gave you extra help). Just shows you what consistent original and thoughtful clueing can do. Very workmanlike Friday that gave me a throwback challenge for a change, thanks!
Still no love for ER Nurses doing triage? (Not that it fit).
Thanks to Agatha again for MEWS (also just finished rereading The Mysterious Affair at STYLES, Lol)
Just learned about POTOROOS—cute little guy!
A tough but rather enjoyable puzzle when all was filled and done.

Just the sort of challenge I love--when I'm not severely sleep-deprived. I did not think I would finish, or even get halfway. I may have cried a little.

But I did it! Somehow. Thank you, Kelly Morenus, for a lovely puzzle, even though it almost killed me.

Editors: MORE OF THIS PLEASE.

Gotta work on my patience, I gave up on this one. Kudos to those who stayed the course.

The Monty Python “argument” sketch where Michael Palin mistakenly enters the “getting hit in the head lessons” room comes to mind. Happy Friday!

I expect that others found this one easier than I did. Solved it without assistance, but took over 40 minutes.

Liked the all-purpose clue for HERE. Never heard of a POTOROO, but it was clued in a helpful way. Though why we had TROP rather than PORT (an actual word) or OPRT (alphabetical order)? Perhaps I'll find an answer in the column or comments. Abbreviation for "tropical"?

MEWS was also new to me, but I suppose it has been clued as it was today in previous puzzles and I've simply forgotten.

Liked the long entries, with the exception of DEALERSSHOE, which felt a bit forced. Yes, a dealer might deal from a shoe, so I suppose the device being used is the DEALER'S SHOE. But the singular noun DEALINGSHOE seems more natural, as does the plural noun DEALERSHOES.  The possessive construction seems a bit odd to me, but, hey, the grid came together well enough.

The adjacent plural pairs RADARS/AGARS and DIAPERS/STYLES also felt a but "kludgy", with RADARS and AGARS seeming especially forced.

All in all, a solid Friday challenge for me, but not a puzzle that especially stood out.
For all those who have complaints about MEWS, what else would fit given the clue?

Typical tough Friday workout for me. Had to look up a couple of things, but mostly just working the crosses until something finally dawned on me. And... three of the 15 letter grid-spanners were debuts, but none of them were terribly unusual phrases. A bit surprised by that. 

Puzzle finds today I'll put in a reply. 

...
KILLER puzzle...in the best way possible. Tough as the toughest Saturday. Who could not want that? Who Saturdays?? Yayyyy!

Once again, I'm so grateful for the "Easy Mode" clues by Christina Iverson, they "make it make sense". (I needed twenty two of them.)

Kudos to those of you who wrestled with this very hard puzzle on your own!
Brutally tough. Took years off my life.

This was quite tough…finished around 25% above my average time, but with no help, so I’m quite satisfied. Beyond that, it gave me an excuse to sit a bit longer on my deck, looking out over the woods, watching the birds, and drinking coffee before I have to get down to the day’s work…so, thank you for the challenge, Rich Katz.

Hoo, that was a tough one. Had to put it away and return to the SE several times. Somehow excavated HOGANS from the recesses of my memory and that cracked it open. 

I thought my XWSTATS were particularly indicative of how tough this one was for me: 

"You solved this 17:18 (90.8%) slower than your Friday average. This is 26:16 slower than your personal best Friday. This is currently your 3rd slowest Friday puzzle of all time."

OooooKayyyy.  
I started in the NW, which was deceptively gettable...and then...
Oof.  Good challenge!!

I took PLANE Geometry in 9th grade; this clue does not plainly (ha ha) jibe with the usage I'm accustomed to, but I'm sure Barry A will defend it, so I'll move on.  

I know of the POTTO, but the name POTOROO is, I admit, higher on the Cute-meter.  

AUDI makes a Q3, I see.  I don't go by "make and model."  In my book it's red car, blue car, black car.... and Manual or Automatic.  I loved my Volkswagen station wagon (manual) that DHubby cruelly dubbed "too old" and sold out from under me....35+ years ago, but still....   

Anyone else wear a SKORT back in the day?  As in, 1958? 
Ugh-ly style.

That's about it....  Stay safe this weekend, Puzzlers!
Great Friday! Grazie!
I watched the DEVO documentary on Netflix last night. I was never really into them, but liked their videos on MTV, and rather enjoyed the documentary.

One more puzzle find. A Sunday from December 25, 1994 by Randolph Ross with the title "Seasons Greetings." All of the clues for the theme answers started with "Seasons greetings from..." And here are some examples: "Athens" KALACHRISTOUGENNA "Seoul" CHOOKSUNGTAN "Rome" BUONNATALE "Oslo" GLEDELIGJUL "Paris" JOYEUXNOEL "Madrid" FELIZNAVIDAD "Oahu" MELEKALIKIMAKA "Dublin" NOLLAIGSHONADHUIT Whew. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/25/1994&h=100a ....

When I worked at the Atlanta Summer Games, female staff were issued khaki SKORTS. They referred to using the bathroom while wearing them as "The Olympic Challenge." So maybe not so much leisure? Asking for a friend.

Has anyone here ever heard of a POTOROO before today?

Thanks to Will Shortz for making the DEVO band cross gettable for me by providing an "only these letters" clue for POTOROO.  Otherwise the band and the animal could have been just about anything.  How do I know it's a Will Shortz clue?  Because it has WS's fingerprints all over it.  Y'all also see that, don't you?

I love MAKE IT MAKE SENSE.  I've never heard that phrase, but it's quite colorful.  These days, though, it might be very frustrating.  There are some things that just can't be made to make sense -- with a whole slew of them coming out of the Oval Office every single half hour.

Cute, deceptive clue for HERE.

Thought for the day -- and it's a thought I've often had as both a solver and a constructor.  THERE IS ABSOLUTELY NO CLUE FOR "EKE" THAT'S NOT A SLAM DUNK.  I have never, ever seen one.  Have you, Lewis?  None of our most elite constructors and most devious clue-writers have ever been able to find one.  So if EKE, EKES or EKED is in the puzzle, you're going to have a gimme.

I found this an entertaining and not particularly hard Friday themeless.
Another excellent tough Friday. I love it when I’m 10 minutes over my average. Thank you.

I was surprised to learn that a DEALERSSHOE sometimes contains more than one deck when I read the book "Bringing Down the House: The Inside Story of Six MIT Students Who Took Vegas for Millions" by journalist Ben Mezrich.

This is a true-life story about how a bunch of MIT gonzos took over Las Vegas and won a fortune playing blackjack.

The book was made into a movie called "21" which is a feast for the eyes, with all the casino glitz. But I wouldn't recommend the movie because it contains a bunch of scary stuff that wasn't in the (supposedly) non-fiction book.

What a satisfying hard Friday. A slew of wrong guesses put me into overtime. 1A "arf," because I thought 7D "afro"; 10A had "hall"; 19A "nolo." Only after deleting all of these and looking at the long across answers anew was I able to crack it.

I enjoyed this, and delighted to have completed in average time without lookups, despite being held up by having “dealer’s edge” and “cut in two” in place for quite a while. 

The clueing was very satisfying, and the absence of US brand and trivia references a refreshing change.
Best clue of the day: 22A
boom! the theme answers were great, and the crosses perfect. Friday level fun all the way! Thanks!

Was relieved to see this one rated Very Hard. Because it could have been just my lack of sleep or the fact that I had a 2 hour interruption in the middle of my solve. Probably all of the above. Completed without help in almost double my usual time and over double my usually humbling.

It was SKORTS finally broke the grid open for me. An article of clothing I haven't worn since childhood. 

Thank you for a hard one!
Now THAT was a great workout!  Thanks for a worthy Friday challenge!
Surprised myself by completing this, especially since I'm watching Halftime Report on cnbc (with the sound on).

Oof, I finally got the update where the comments open in the Games app and I can now understand all the complaints. Blinding white that doesn’t respect my Dark Mode setting. Inability to easily swap between the puzzle and comments to make sense of someone’s “25D” comment. Who thought this was a good idea?
It’s been a long week and I just don’t have it in me for this one. You win, constructor.
These words were much too difficult today. I understand two of them and it was just too complicated. I understand not making them all common knowledge, but please do at least some.

I was halfway through when I sat back down to finish after polishing off a bottle of sparkling wine (I'm on vacation!). Completed the second half in 10 minutes. I'm a big Devo fan, so that was a gimme, but I am flummoxed how I cut through the second half like butter. I am a SLOW solver, too. Magic wine...

a/ can you see the future
see through this wave of now 
to the destination 
the d/ temple
where they name 
the forbidden secret
and who is behind a/ it
i want to know
i want to know the way 
i want to look in

EMEND twice this week! So, "emend" means to make adjustments, for the better, to a text; "amend" means to make adjustments, for the better (we hope), to almost anything else, even a Constitution. Unless you're Roman Catholic, you're in Spain, and it's Ash Wednesday, ca. 1550: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-WPKE-uUOXc&list=RD-WPKE-uUOXc&start_radio=1 (Don't worry, you can have your cava after Mass.) (Excuse to link one of my favorite pieces of Renaissance choral polyphony.)
Does using an abbreviation in the definition no longer indicate an abbreviation in the answer i.e. e.g.?

Ouch.

But in a good way.
A good hard one!   well done.
Bravo! This was a worthy Friday and such a fun ride. I thought I would need to resort to Google to crack this baby open, but perseverence paid.
*rubs eyes* Is it Saturday today...?

Heh- heh! heh-heh! 

The puzzle had PEEN in it, Beavis!

Interesting late answer search. I went and did a search and... CONDUCTOR, INSTRUCTOR, ABDUCTOR and a couple of others - have been answers or parts of answers in a number of puzzles, but... the string of letters CONSTRUCTOR has never been in any puzzle. Hmmm... .... but, in a roundabout way that search led me to an interesting puzzle - a Sunday from February 22, 2009 by Barry C. Silk and Doug Peterson. Theme answers in that one: BRAINSTORMTHEMEIDEAS DEVELOPBESTENTRIES CONSTRUCTSUITABLEGRID RESEARCHANDWRITECLUES SENDPUZZLETOEDITOR SEEBYLINEINNEWSPAPER Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/22/2009&h=59a ....
Not a fan of how the link to the column now opens within the games app instead of opening the NYT App. Why the change and can we set it back to the “ol’ timey” way of opening the column?
Started this last night and just finished a few minutes ago…😅 phew. Now that’s a Friday puzzle! Thank you, Kelly, for the solid workout for my brain cells 💪🏼🧠
Tough puzzle, but thank you for introducing me to the potoroo! Lovely creature.
And this one is just right.
How do I open the tips in a browser instead of in the app?
I really wanted British row houses to be pews.
Well, I learned a lot.

Kudos to the constructor for an awesome workout! My actual time was around 35 minutes but I opened and closed the puzzle throughout the day, as the aha moments arrived. I don't often blow my own horn, but I'm proud that I solved without any hints or the "check puzzle" facility.
Also, to Sean McGowan-your column today elicited 3 out-loud laughs! Thanks, I needed them.

Fun, challenging puzzle. Took me 53:11 unaided.

I definitely had to rework several sections as it became clear that something just wasn't working.

Appreciate that there wasn't a lot of "trivia," but dang, I've never heard of mews and would never have guessed it.
Excellent puzzle. No obscure college sports references. Lovely
Took all day on and off to complete. Loved all the spanner phrases. You can GIVETH me puzzles like this to chew on any Friday.

One more puzzle find. I'll re-post this one tomorrow. A Sunday from August 8, 1982 by Mary Virginia Orna with the title: "Latin Rhythms" Six 23-letter grid spanning theme answers: BYTHEPULCHRITUDINOUSSEA DANCINGINTHETENEBROSITY YOULLNEVERAMBULATEALONE MYOLDKENTUCKYHABITATION IVEGOTYOUSUBCUTANEOUSLY ILLSEEYOUINMYPHANTASMAS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=8/8/1982&h=36a ...
Can someone please help me understand DEALERSSHOE?

As a 2yr puzzler, I've learned my place in the heirarchy and instead of yelling at my own lack of knowledge, I now start Thu/Fri/Sat sans-help in the AM and then let them stew, and enjoy the augmented finish in the PM. Rarely a goldstar but still fun to ponder clues during the day while working and finish with a tasty beverage. Cheers!

Tough Friday puzzle but I managed to finish without lookups only 6 minutes longer than my average. 

Rating:
8/10

While not as horrendous as other Fridays this was still a drudge for me. Very disheartening when you know a decent amount of the clues and still can't solve without looking up a long list of esoteric trivia whose only real world application is to solve a crossword puzzle. I don't mind looking up and learning but a crossword for me is supposed to be engaging entertainment not a slog.

On mobile it wouldn’t solve. I eventually looked up the answer and it was completely right. I checked every square. Eventually I chose to “check puzzle” and it told e the top left vowel was incorrect (it wasn’t). Only after I changed it and then changed it back did the puzzle solve.

Frustrating, but fortunately I was not on a streak or anything. Coincidentally I lost wordle today :(
Wow, I haven't been that stumped in several years ... finished, but well over my average!

DEMS makes more sense to me than LEFT, but mostly because I don't think the Democrats are really all that Left.

I think if answers are going to be given, it would be helpful if something from each quadrant was examined.

Even though I was able to get nearly all of the longer entries, It still had to struggle to finish this one.  A couple of entries did not seem to make sense to me, even though they turned out to be correct, until I read the summary.  Took a while, tough Saturday, as it should be.

Apparently being tipsy is a good thing when solving Friday puzzles. I thought this one took longer than it did but I ended up under 20 minutes. I dredged the potoroo out of memory which helped. Enjoyable puzzle for my retirement day.
In pen without lookups, but this one turned out to be as smudgy of a mess in the end as I can remember. Very fun, though, and a gratifying solve.
Can anyone tell me why the world play column won’t open in a new tab anymore?

I don't often do more than skim the column,  today's was at least as entertaining as the puzzle. And I really enjoyed this puzzle. It was just the right mixture of totally opaque clues and helpful crosses to make it nice and chewy.

Ok, this was it. I’ll sometimes get some help on a clue here or there—rarely.  But I haven’t run into a puzzle I just couldn’t solve in probably five years. I had to turn on auto check to get through this one. And it’s only a Friday!  Not sure why this was so impossible for me, but o guess it had to happen eventually.
Puzzles shouldn’t be unsolvable. Thanks anyway

It's rare that being Australian actually helps, but POTOROO was a given for me and my British TV upbringing means that MEWS wasn't that hard either. 

But the rest of the puzzle? Woah.

Also I can tell you that GOOSES means something ENTIRELY different to the Brits.
This one didn't do it for me; I found it quite annoying and, after some of the explanations, I'd call it too-clever-by-half, but only if I agreed that it was clever.
Ummm, yeah.  40 across is factually incorrect.  You see through your pupil, not your iris.  Your iris actually blocks light.
Fun puzzle. Having StyeS for 29 down hung me up in the east all day. Yikes.
Diapers don’t snap.

“myself and a team of friends will be participating . . .” “myself . . . will be participating . . .” Oh, dear. Try this: “A team of friends and I will be participating . . .” And when you’re up for it: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reflexive_verb You’ll please the Grey Lady. And her admirers.

"it wasn’t until the 2000 presidential election that news broadcasts began uniformly denoting Democrats with the color blue and Republicans with red."

What? I was alive before 00 and this is definitely not correct.
@Steve L I've been slower than my average three times this week, including today. I don't know if it's been a tough week, or a low mental energy one. I fear the latter.

@Steve L

30% over average here.

@Emma
I was stymied by (l)OOSE and (l)IVETH, which made perfect sense to me.

@Francis 
This is a simplistic answer.  
Going into bankruptcy can protect you from creditors and if you can reorganize while protected, perhaps you can reopen. 

Perhaps someone will come along and explain in more depth.
@Marshall Walthew I had trouble with this one because I was thinking about individuals, not businesses. I wanted it to be something like "recovered".

@Marshall Walthew you didn't need to feel any kind of churlish to chime in here. In fact you were being way too kind. I see bk deals all the time and have honestly never seen a company "close" while in Chapter 11. If they did, they'd be giving up any revenue thereby putting themselves in a much worse position. The whole point of filing for bk is to reorg while you're open.

@Francis "Cava" is on Spanish champagne labels but I thought it was an adjective, not the type of wine. TIL for me.

The Iota clue refers literally to the three (Greek) I symbole.

@Francis

You've heard of CAVA. It's appeared in these puzzles before. I also often believe I've never heard of stuff I have simply forgotten.

@Francis

Oh, yeah, HOGANS. It was one of the many things that I had to guess. What the phi are those? I know I can Google but humor me, please.

@Francis

27d: Think of the carving on fraternity house IOTA IOTA IOTA.

@Francis

Being brave for me 🥰

@Andrzej

And, I missed a bit, and in a kind of embarrassing way.  

Hogans seem to be more than I said, and they are primarily Navaho, which I did not know.  They apparently had a more honored place in Navaho tradition.

To be honest, I know embarrassingly little about pre-European North America.  The word HOGANS itself was really, really distant.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Ed

Normally, I'd agree.

But if you look at the appositions here, they're kind of brilliant in their respective pairings, as Kelly suggests in her notes.

17A is precisely the beginning of the response to 14A, while if you separate 49A and 52A with nothing more than a hyphen, you have a rather portentous request from a pol or a CEO.

I'd stop short of claiming that all four spanners together "tell a little story" unless your imagination is shaped like a Mobius strip, but still...primo spanning for this solver.
@Mark The scale of difficulty must have been high.

@Mark

They snuck in through the asp hole?

@Mark, M. C. Escher was famous for tessellating the PLANE with birds, fish, lizards, horses, and whatnot; I'm sure he could have done the same with snakes. :-)

(There actually is an Escher work titled "Snakes", but it is not a tessellation; the snake doesn't look particularly planar there, due to the shading and layering.)

@Red Carpet
I hope you don't end up wandering for 40 years.

@SBK in TO

One would think that with all the manna the big guy rained down upon them, that they (if the "Trinity" isn't "they", what is?) could have also sent down a couple of GPS devices.

@Patrick J.

You know, that's very interesting, because I distinctly remember political maps used to use blue for GOP and red for the Democrats.

I distinctly remember watching returns in the 1968 election, and I could swear that when Humphrey won New York, the state went bright red on their display.  And when Nixon won nearly the entire West, I remember it being a solid blue.

And I thought, at the time, that it was an insult to the Democrats.  Because at that time "red" meant "communist".  

"Better dead than red" was the slogan.

Does anyone else remember any of this?

@Francis In 2004, my Cartography teacher was excited about them showing two colors on the election maps. Guess maybe he did not notice it in 2000. I didn't have a functioning TV in 2000 so I would not have noticed it. I've always hated red-blue-purple. It seems too simplistic.

@Patrick J.

Also, labeling Democrats as the left seems funny to a European - at best they are centrist by our standards, and on some issues they sit firmly on the right.

What even is left and right anymore? The "right" seem to want the government to ban things and control people's lives all over the place, while the "left" becomes more of a cultural or social club without any political foundation at all. Time for an updated way of defining how everyone hates each other, I think!

@Patrick J.

I lay the blame for this on a graphic designer at the NY Times.
The eye-catching displays of election results, (perhaps 2000?), based on new computerized data quickly became the way news organizations addressed the maps and the politics.

As you point out, the new usage overturned centuries of previous practice.  Royal Blue for authoritarian conservatism and Red for peoples' revolution.  (Consider the flags chosen for the USSR and Mao's China.)
@Al  in Pittsburgh who cares what color it is so long as you realize that government is a necessary monopolistic evil, that ought to be minimized.

@Andrzej
Using the term 'left' in US politics seems to be relatively recent, isn't it? I've been doing a double-take for a while.

@Francis
I definitely recall that, when the news outlets started referring to blue and red, it took me a couple of election cycles to remember which was which. I’d like to know who decided to standardize graphics display on those two colors.

@Jane Wheelaghan

Right? Up until recently The Dems were akin to the mainstream European right, while the Republicans looked like something straight out of the l00ny bin. I guess as the Rs move into previously uncharted right-wing
E
X
Tremism, the Dems are moving left.

@fionatimes
One of the best lines from The Mary Tyler Moore Show: the idiot news reader claims his favourite colour is "plaid."
@Francis --- I don't personally remember, but this article makes your memories very plausible: https://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/when-republicans-became-red-democrats-became-blue-104176297/

@Bob

Anarchy doesn't work very well.

Generally, without a strong government you wind up with small violent groups led by warlords competing for control.  The population suffers as the economy is ruined.

Look at Somalia and Haiti for examples.

@Bob

And why can't a private business be a "monopolistic evil" which ought to be minimized?
You took the words out of my mouth!

@dutchiris

😂 Yes, the Thrill of Victory!

@dutchiris I see that no one has posted a link to Angela Lansbury's performance from Dear World, the musical based on The Madwomsn of Chaillot. Here you go: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KKQMRhBNr2g Enjoy.

@SBK in TO
it just popped into my head as I read @dutchiris's comment!

@Mike 
Euroften upset and we hear accounts, dime after dime, of problems like this. Next time you should teller exactly what you want: laundry money.

@dutchiris
Yes, he should give the teller no quarter.

@Kevin lots of us hate it. When it first appeared there was much complaining. It seems they are just going to wait until we just ‘accept’ our fate. There is no indication that the puzzle technical team has user feedback in its workflow. 

Almost certainly by doing it this way the app becomes a self standing entity that can be tested and used without dependencies (eg which is the default browser of the user).

@Kevin
It took me two times scrolling through the Wordplay column to figure out where the comments had gone. When I went back to the puzzle I finally saw the teeny-tiny comments box. I will say, though, that I like that I can now see the comments in dark mode, which I prefer.
@Kevin - You can open Wordplay in the main NYT app by searching Wordplay.  Leave the puzzle open in the games app.
@jennie another option is to stay in the comments in the games app, and open the puzzle in the NYT app.  It will be filled in with your answers

I'm generally not easy to offend, but applying stereotypes to me does the trick. Eastern Europeans, and Slavs in particular, are the continent's pariahs. When thinking of us, Westerners often wave their hand in a general easterly direction, dismissing "those people" as a homogenous horde of no-goodniks. In fact the blues are all in favor of tariffs and other regressive taxes, which has been redistributing wealth from the poor to the wealthy since 1975, when inflation overtook wage growth and has been eroding middle class wages for nearly all that time.

@ALH
Interesting. As Jane says, not here in the UK. I have a 5 month old grandson and nappies here are definitely of the sticky tape variety still.
@ALH Well that might have been a game-changer 34 years ago. Had no idea. Cool.

@Helen Wright

Surely those are not cloth??

@ALH
I still buy cotton DIAPERS because they're great for kitchen/cooking tasks (and later for window-washing, etc.)  Snaps would be a pain in this case.

Not many use cloth nappies any more. Not good for environment of course. It used to be said that the energy needed to wash and dry cloth nappies was equal to that needed to make and dispose of non-cloth nappies, but don’t know if it’s true.

@Steve L
 Nope. The vast majority of nappies used here are disposable. My very eco minded daughter in law did the homework pre birth, assuming reusable would be better, but was surprised to find that by the time you factor in water/electricity usage for soaking, washing and drying, plus the initial expense of purchasing reusables,  it’s much more expensive and not that eco friendly. I think our utility costs are much higher in the UK, plus we can’t dry out doors for much of the year due to our wet climate, so it’s either an expensive dryer/heat pod or wet nappies constantly drying in the home, leading to condensation, mould etc.  Hence the reusable market here is tiny and frankly only for those wealthy enough to afford the outlay and running costs.

@Jane Wheelaghan
I bought a bag of Fuji apples yesterday,  Hogans are Navajo Tribe homes -- learned that in 4th grade California history.  PEEN is one end of a ball-peen hammer.  I had to look at that one for a minute....  I had it on crosses, but definition didn't come right away.  
My Biblical verb was haVETH, so wanted the energizer to hoist - but that didn't fit, either.

Finally had to look at answer key to get POTOROO and GOOSES.

@Eric
That's right, but given the space, and the W in place, there could not be many choices.  Newer construction may have used the term nowadays, so I gave it a pass.  It's like a neighborhood in the US called "Brookside" or something, when the brook was rerouted and channeled into a pipe!

The plastic undies that one puts on a baby after the cloth diaper is pinned may be a snap-on, but even in the early 80's that was rare.  Elastic waistbands and leg-openings, or diaper covers that used Velcro closures were more often in use.  Anything that makes a frequent chore easier and faster will supersede the older style!
@Helen Wright modern cloth diapers use snaps. That's what I had for my son.

@Lewis I swear...when we go away from a puzzle that is killing us for a while, there are background apps in our noggins that are running, solving, obsessing. What else could possibly explain these moments of enlightenment when a whole section or the whole puzzle suddenly opens up to us??

@APNerd
I think the term you want is not 'background apps' but 'subconscious'.

@Lewis
Perfect description!  Though I did still give 26D a stern look.

@Lewis

For me it’s like painting. I’m a very detailed painter so when I’m working I am looking close up at minor details in color and shape. It’s only when I step back and take a break and take a look at the whole picture do I see, wow, that actually looks like what I was trying to create. So in the same way we can sometimes be overthinking crossword clues and trying to fit in stray letter patterns and guesses, but when we take a break and come back to it we get some big picture inspiration.

@Lewis

I call that moment "The Click".

@Rich in Atlanta As threatened: Two puzzles that had similar but somewhat different tricks. First - a Thursday from September 6, 2012 by Ben Pall. In that one - four sections that had double-wide or double-long squares that had just one letter in them. And... the 'trick' was that in one direction those were just single letters, and in the other direction they were implied to be stacked and used separately by two different answers. One example: PIZZA ______JOINT ROTARY While the down crossings over JOINT were RA(J)A, EB(O)N, AB(I)T, MI(N)X and SET(A). And the other 3 double spaced trick answers were: UNION, BREAK and SPLIT. You need to look at it to really get it. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/6/2012 I'll put the other puzzle in another reply. ...

@Rich in Atlanta As threatened: A Thursday from April 4, 2013 by Corey Rubin. This one is extremely difficult to describe. There were 10 4x4 squares that just contained one letter. And that was a single letter in each of the four crossing answers. One example with an R in the big square - two down crosses: suRe and aRia and the across crosses: niRvana and gRaf. So that R box was a part of four different answers. But then.. Those boxes were placed in a diagonal down direction from left to right and top to bottom. So the big letters in each of those, read together, were: LARGE PRINT Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=4/4/2013 I'm done.
